segments.title: The dreadnought race was three contests at once
segments.summary: Dr. Clarke separates the dreadnought era into an Anglo-German numbers contest, an international design competition, and a South American naval race.
segments.body: The familiar Anglo-German rivalry was a quantitative contest shaped by Germany's risk-fleet theory: build enough ships that Britain would hesitate to fight. At the same time, Britain, Italy, Japan, and the United States competed qualitatively by producing increasingly capable designs, including super-dreadnoughts and the Queen Elizabeth class. Argentina, Brazil, and Chile conducted a further dreadnought competition in South America, making the period broader than a single European rivalry.

evidence.note: The transcript distinguishes the Anglo-German quantitative race and explains Germany's risk-fleet logic.
The transcript identifies a qualitative design race among Britain, Italy, Japan, and America, plus a South American race among Argentina, Brazil, and Chile.
